author: Abdulkader Safi
description: Resize, crop, rotate, compress and convert images in your vault, powered by Safi-Image. - This plugin has not been manually reviewed by Obsidian staff.

manifest.author: Abdulkader Safi
manifest.description: Resize, crop, rotate, compress and convert images in your vault.
manifest.name: ImageMagick

name: ImageMagick

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
A cleanup release on top of 2.0.0. Nothing about editing images changes.

**This one needs Obsidian 1.13 or later.** The settings tab now uses Obsidian's declarative settings API, which landed in 1.13, so your presets show up in settings search. If you are on an older version you keep 2.0.0 and everything still works.

## What changed

- Settings are searchable, and the presets list gets Obsidian's own add and delete controls.
- The image preview now builds its scratch canvas with Obsidian's helper rather than a raw DOM call.
- Each release file is signed on its own, so its build provenance can be checked independently.

## Corrected

The 2.0.0 notes said the plugin ships no WebAssembly. That was wrong: WebP encoding uses a small WebAssembly codec. It is inlined into `main.js` like everything else, so nothing is downloaded and no image leaves your machine, but the claim was not accurate and the README now says so plainly.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
The plugin is now about eight times smaller: `main.js` goes from 6.9 MB to 843 KB.

ImageMagick's WebAssembly build is gone, replaced by [safi-image](https://github.com/Abdulkader-Safi/safi-image), a pure TypeScript image library written for this plugin. Nothing is downloaded at runtime and no image ever leaves your machine, same as before.

The editor is unchanged. Resize, crop, rotate, flip, compress, convert, presets and bulk edit all work as they did.

## Breaking

- **AVIF and HEIC are no longer supported.** Both are patent-encumbered codecs with no pure TypeScript decoder. They are gone from the format list and from the files the plugin will open, so a vault holding `.heic` photos cannot open them here.
- **Progressive JPEG will not open.** Many JPEGs saved from the web are progressive. You get a clear message rather than a broken image, but the file will not load.
- A saved preset using AVIF is dropped, and you fall back to the built-in presets. Set it to WebP or JPEG instead.

## Also in this release

EXIF orientation is applied on open, so a photo shot upright arrives upright, and crop rectangles land where you draw them.

Fixed: the format dropdown in settings offered AVIF even where it could not be produced, which saved a preset the plugin then quietly discarded.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
A cleanup release that clears the issues Obsidian's plugin scorecard flagged. No feature changes.

## Fixed

- **Both scorecard risks are gone.** They were inline `eslint-disable` comments silencing Obsidian's own `ui/sentence-case` rule, which is not allowed. The code changed instead: the file-menu entry is now **Optimize image**, and the strip-metadata description no longer leans on the EXIF and GPS acronyms.
- **Licence is MIT.** The `LICENSE` file still carried the plugin template's 0BSD text while `package.json` and the README both said MIT, so the scorecard reported the wrong licence.
- **Added a contributing guide**, the one hygiene item the scorecard was missing.
- Dropped `setDynamicTooltip()`, deprecated now that slider values are always shown inline.
- Fixed a stale repository URL in `package.json`.

## Known warning

`main.js` is about 6.9 MB, over the 5 MB threshold where Obsidian Sync's Standard plan stops syncing a file. This one cannot be fixed while the plugin ships real ImageMagick. Obsidian only downloads `main.js`, `manifest.json` and `styles.css` when installing a plugin, so the 14 MB WebAssembly engine has to live inside the bundle. It is already gzipped to 5.2 MB, and base64 puts it at 6.9 MB. Brotli only reaches 5.2 MB after encoding, still over the line.

It affects syncing the plugin's own files between devices on the Standard plan. It does not affect installing the plugin, which works normally on every plan and platform.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
First release of ImageMagick for Obsidian: resize, crop, rotate, compress and convert images without leaving your vault. Nothing is uploaded anywhere, and nothing is fetched at runtime.

This is an Obsidian port of my [VS Code extension](https://github.com/Abdulkader-Safi/vscode-extensions-ImageMagick).

## Features

- **Resize** with width and height inputs, an aspect-ratio lock, and percentage presets.
- **Crop** by dragging a region on the preview, or by typing exact coordinates.
- **Rotate and flip**, with 90° presets and a free-angle slider.
- **Compress** with a quality slider for lossy formats.
- **Convert** between JPEG, PNG, WebP, GIF, TIFF and BMP (plus AVIF where the bundled engine supports it).
- **Live preview** with an output size estimate, so you can see what a save will cost before you make it.
- **Presets**: right-click an image and pick "Optimize with preset" to write optimized output straight into the vault, no editor. Presets hold a format, quality, max size, and metadata-strip choice, and are editable in the plugin settings.
- **Bulk edit**: select several images, tune the pipeline on one, and apply it to all of them.

## Usage

Right-click any image in the file explorer and choose **Optimize with ImageMagick**, or run **ImageMagick: Open image** from the command palette.

## Notes

`main.js` is around 7 MB because Obsidian only downloads `main.js`, `manifest.json` and `styles.css` when installing a plugin. The 14 MB ImageMagick WebAssembly engine therefore has to ship inside the bundle. It is gzipped and inlined, and the renderer expands it the first time you open an image.

Requires Obsidian 1.7.2 or later. Works on desktop and mobile.
